summary: A new and dangerous drug, Sweet Beyond, dominated the Council's second session. After refusing an exclusive deal with its alchemist, the guards stormed the Fin's hidden laboratory by force, exposing both the criminal syndicate behind its trade and the grim secret of its origin in the harvested glands of Sirens. Meanwhile, the Scourge of Coden continued murdering golden-eyed women unchecked, as the Council declined a curfew to protect the taverns' coin. Elsewhere, Baron Lychen's bid for First Judge collapsed under suspicion of extremist sympathies, Aontas finally received its due investment, Dualak's gruesome new play was banned outright, and the kingdom's youngest laborers won new protections.

dilemmas.title: Romero's Banned Spectacle
dilemmas.prompt: Inspired by the macabre events of the Konfurtahn war, playwright Romero has written an extremely innovative and gory play, in which horrid monsters drain the blood of people, turning them into walking corpses hunting for the flesh of the living. To increase realism, Romero uses animal blood on stage as a special effect. The play is creating quite a scandal in **Dualak**, scaring the commoners to tears, and many Dualakians are asking us to ban the play immediately. Do we prohibit the staging of the play?

resolution.narrative: We send our guards to dissuade Romero from staging his ridiculous play. Despite the playwright's protests, we are sure he will find something more meaningful and less gory to write about.

dilemmas.title: A Law for the Laborers
dilemmas.prompt: The Queen's grant of regional autonomy has led to a wave of new infrastructure and construction projects across Ankist. With this surge, labor has become largely unregulated, with workers enduring harsh shifts, including children exposed to hazardous conditions. The Tribune of the People urges us to implement labor regulations on construction sites to prevent further exploitation. Shall we answer the Tribune's call?

resolution.narrative: With greater power comes greater responsibility, not greater exploitation. We cannot allow the drive for progress to go hand-in-hand with the reckless exploitation of the most vulnerable in our society. A new law is enacted to protect the rights of laborers at construction sites, limiting work hours to sixty per week and prohibiting the presence of children under the age of thirteen.

dilemmas.title: Lychen's Fall From Grace
dilemmas.prompt: The election for the next First Judge, the highest judicial office in the Court of Lybra, is fast approaching. The clear frontrunner is Baron Lychen, a noble known for his decisiveness, charisma, and ability to bear pressure. However, during a recent event, Lychen was seen greeting the crowd with a gesture that many associated with the Orden Baleanth, a traditionalist group that venerates the old ways of Ankist—upholding values that are deeply nationalist, exclusionary, and hostile toward the Enkhali. While Lychen denies having made the gesture, the event has raised concerns about his impartiality as First Judge, with many calling for his candidacy to be rejected. Do we disqualify Lychen from the election?

resolution.narrative: We cannot appoint a First Judge whose impartiality is in question: Lychen is disqualified and the second runner, Bradan—known for his moral integrity—is named First Judge. Lychen leaves the court once again denying the accusations, but ends with a strange rant, lamenting a kingdom where appeasing minorities and control of thought matter more than years of loyal service.

dilemmas.title: Aontas Gets Its Due
dilemmas.prompt: Our regional investments are yielding significant rewards—especially in areas directly under our influence. The only region not reaping these benefits is Aontas. As the seat of central power, its development has always been the responsibility of the regents. The Tribune is now urging the Council to invest in this region as well, to ensure that Lybra, our capital, does not fall behind the kingdom's other great cities. Should we heed the Tribune's call?

resolution.narrative: Maintaining goodwill with the people of the capital—as well as the Queen and other influential figures—seems the wiser course, even if it comes at the expense of our coffers. If we persist in seeking more power only for ourselves, eventually someone may ask us to pay with our heads. By honoring the Tribune's request ourselves, we secure not only public favor but also the respect of our peers through a deft political maneuver.

dilemmas.title: The Council Refuses Sweet Beyond
dilemmas.prompt: A new substance, Sweet Beyond, is spreading in Roinn, loosening inhibitions and heightening sexual drive and pleasure. Gael, the Soladian alchemist who created it, approaches us with an intriguing offer: to sell Sweet Beyond to us exclusively, granting us the chance to profit from it. However, we still know very little about this substance, and exposing the Council to the market of something so unknown could be risky. Do we partake in the sale of Sweet Beyond?

resolution.narrative: We decide not to partake in the trade of such an unknown and potentially dangerous substance. Unfortunately, some shady criminals in Solad do not share our caution.

dilemmas.title: No Curfew for Coden's Killer
dilemmas.prompt: A prostitute is found murdered in the slums of **Coden**, the third in a month. All victims have suffered horrendous cuts and share an eerie similarity: yellow eyes, reminiscent of Queen Serena's gaze. Some suspect a deranged killer is at large. The Tribune suggests implementing a nighttime curfew to protect the people, but this would hurt the business of taverns and brothels in **Coden**. Do we impose a curfew?

resolution.narrative: Without a working economy, there can be no security. We refuse to impose a curfew: the killings must be isolated incidents. After all, so many of our people die every day in Konfurtah, and we didn't close a single brothel for them.

dilemmas.title: The Guards Storm the Laboratory
dilemmas.prompt: Thanks to an informer, the Covenant of Whispers discovered the location of the main laboratory where the Fin is producing Sweet Beyond. We could send our soldiers to take it over, but we should expect heavy, armed resistance. Or we could try to pay off the guarding goons to leave the laboratory undefended, though it could be expensive to convince them to betray their criminal family. Do we try to pay off the goons?

resolution.narrative: We send in the guards. The clash with the goons from a criminal organization is not bloodless: many guards lose their lives. Yet, we severely cripple the Fin, even if they'll surely find other criminal activities to strive for. Meanwhile, by taking over the main laboratory of Sweet Beyond, we finally learn where it comes from.

dilemmas.title: A Sheep for a Siren
dilemmas.prompt: Hummel, a cousin to the ruling ducal family of **Solad**, tamed a Siren. Then, he engaged in lewd acts with the animal night and day. Exasperated by the cries of both, the shepherd Karich killed the Siren. Hummel demands that Karich be put to death, but the traditional law of retaliation says that, to serve justice, it would be enough to allow Hummel to kill one of the shepherd's sheep. Do we sentence Karich to death?

resolution.narrative: Karich's was an act of violence toward an animal, and it is therefore the opinion of the Council that the retaliation should strike another animal. Hummel will only be allowed to choose and kill one of the shepherd's sheep and take possession of the carcass as compensation.
